factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the CESG matches 20% of annual RESP contributions up to $2,500 per beneficiary per year, which implies a 20% grant on the first $2,500 contributed per child per year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the CESG provides a maximum annual grant of $500, based on 20% of up to $2,500 contributed per beneficiary per year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states: “Lifetime CESG maximum per child is $7,200,” satisfying the requirement to state the CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that CESG catch-up is limited to $5,000 annually and implies this can recapture carried-forward CESG, but it does not explicitly explain that a $5,000 contribution per child in one year can generate up to $1,000 of CESG due to unused grant room. factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CESG basics, annual limits, lifetime maximums, and catch-up contribution limits, but it does not state the specific rule that to receive CESG at ages 16–17 the child must have had either $2,000 contributed before turning 15 or $100 contributed in any four prior years. factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states: “RESP lifetime contribution limit per child is $50,000,” which matches the required lifetime contribution limit per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains CESG basics (20% match and $7,200 lifetime maximum) but never calculates or states that $36,000 of contributions are needed to maximize the $7,200 CESG. It also does not include the explicit equation $7,200 ÷ 20% = $36,000.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes the $50,000 lifetime RESP contribution limit and the $7,200 lifetime CESG maximum, but it does not state that up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 minus $36,000 CESG-eligible contributions) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant. factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the contributor claims the spousal RRSP deduction at the contributor’s higher marginal rate, but it does not explicitly state that the deduction is immediate and at the contributor’s marginal rate in both cases (own RRSP and spousal RRSP). factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP is contributed to by one spouse but owned by the other (the annuitant), and it notes the 3-year attribution rule where withdrawals within three years are taxed to the contributor, implying that after the attribution period withdrawals are taxed in the annuitant/spouse’s hands.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)

This exemption is not discussed anywhere in the spousal RRSP/RRIF section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that contributing to a spousal RRSP can allow the higher-income spouse to claim the deduction now while withdrawals are taxed to the lower-income spouse in retirement, creating an income-splitting opportunity if the spouse is in a lower tax bracket.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple precise Canadian tax terms, including RRSP, spousal RRSP, RESP, CESG, TFSA, attribution (3-year attribution rule), and marginal tax rates in Ontario. These terms are used in context rather than relying only on generic language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional financial planning tone without product sales marketing language or vague recommendations

presentation-quality.explanation: The response gives a clear recommended allocation strategy (tax reserves first, then steady RESP to maximize CESG, then RRSP contributions, then TFSA with emergency fund in TFSA) and also recommends a hybrid RESP approach plus spousal RRSP when spouse income is lower. It also explains TFSA usage at a high level (e.g., “Use the TFSA as a flexible, tax-free growth vehicle”).
